policy_statement: Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote construction of ULE or NZE buildings.
mechanism_description: Incentives encourage developers to build ULE/NZE buildings, lowering ongoing energy consumption and reducing operational emissions from building energy use.
typology_evidence_quote: "Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote construction of ULE or NZE buildings."
primary_causal_pathway: Building design + operations changes → ↓ energy demand (and often ↑ on-site renewables/efficient electrification) → ↓ fossil energy use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public buildings are planned, designed, constructed, and commissioned to ultra-low energy or net-zero energy performance (high-performance envelope, efficient HVAC/lighting, controls, commissioning, and potentially on-site renewables and electrification).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved thermal resistance/airtightness reduces heat transfer; efficient equipment and controls reduce electricity and fuel required to maintain indoor conditions; on-site renewable generation displaces grid electricity; electrification can shift end-use from combustion to lower-carbon electricity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower annual site and source energy use and/or reduced carbon intensity of supplied energy, resulting in reduced operational CO₂e emissions over the building’s life.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational energy consumption (and/or cleaner supplied energy) leading to lower greenhouse gas emissions from building operations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Net-zero/ULE design reduces operational energy demand and/or supplies low-carbon energy, which directly reduces ongoing GHG emissions from building operations—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such buildings can improve resilience (e.g., better thermal comfort during outages) and resource efficiency (lower energy input for same service), the mechanism’s defining and largest climate-relevant effect is emissions reduction from operations, which maps most directly to Mitigation under Rule 1.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “net-zero” is achieved mainly via purchasing offsets or RECs without real operational reductions, the realized mitigation impact could be weaker; however, the mechanism as defined (“ultra-low or net-zero energy” design/delivery reducing operational energy use) remains fundamentally mitigation-focused.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy centered on net-zero/ultra-low-energy building design and delivery aims to reduce operational energy use and associated emissions, so Mitigation is consistently the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy relies on envelope efficiency, electrification, on-site PV, district energy, or procurement of clean electricity (and any adaptation co-benefits) must be determined from policy-level evidence and is not set here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “net-zero” is met primarily through off-site offsets/RECs rather than actual demand reduction or clean supply, the mitigation effect can be overstated; some projects may also be motivated by resilience/thermal safety, but those are typically co-benefits rather than the dominant pathway for this mechanism.
key_indicators: "Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives"; "promote the construction"; "ULE or NZE buildings"
co_benefits: Potential adaptation co-benefits (not explicit): improved passive survivability during extreme heat and outages; reduced peak load stress.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Non-monetary incentives” is broad (could include expedited permitting, recognition, density bonuses), but still fits the incentive-program category; no explicit regulatory requirement is described.
